title: Widgets
objectives: Explain 'everything is a widget' and immutability

sections.markdown: In Flutter, the UI is built from widgets — immutable configuration objects describing part of the UI. Layout, styling, and even the app itself are widgets composed into a tree.
Widgets are like a blueprint, not the building. Flutter rebuilds blueprints cheaply on every frame; the heavy, persistent objects (Elements/RenderObjects) are kept and reused behind the scenes.
Putting expensive work in build(). build() can run 60+ times/second — keep it pure and cheap, and hoist constant subtrees into const widgets so Flutter can skip them.
Favor composition over deep inheritance: build small widgets and compose them. Use const constructors everywhere possible to cut rebuilds.

flashcards.back: No — widgets are immutable configuration; the mutable part lives in State (for StatefulWidget) and the Element tree.
Stateless has no internal mutable state; Stateful has a State object updated via setState.
Const widgets are canonicalized and can be skipped during rebuilds, improving performance.

quiz.explain: Widgets are immutable descriptions; Elements/RenderObjects hold the mutable state.

options: UI changes over time via internal state
quiz.explain: Stateful widgets manage mutable state that triggers rebuilds.
Const widgets are reused and skipped during rebuilds.

challenges.text: Build a profile card by composing Stateless widgets.
Convert a Stateless counter to Stateful with setState.
Extract a rebuilding subtree into a const widget and verify fewer rebuilds.
Build a reusable, themable component with named constructors and slots (child builders).

interviewQuestions: What does 'everything is a widget' mean?
Why and when use const constructors?
How does composition beat inheritance in Flutter?
